summary: DeVault argues that programmers are too focused on usability at the expense of utility when designing tools. He draws an analogy between professional tools in other fields—race cars, mechanic's toolsets—and programming tools, noting that professional-grade tools inherently require more skill. He contends that since programmers invest years learning their craft, spending extra time learning powerful but less intuitive tools is a worthwhile trade-off. The solution to steep learning curves isn't dumbing down tools, but fostering a culture of mentorship where senior engineers help juniors master more productive tooling.
key_points: Utility and usability are distinct attributes of tools, and optimizing for one can come at the expense of the other
Many programmers today fetishize usability at the expense of utility, preferring approachable tools over powerful ones
Professional programmers already invest years learning their craft, so a steeper learning curve on tools is a minor additional cost
The answer to complex tooling isn't simplification but mentorship—senior engineers teaching juniors to use powerful tools
Tools that are harder to learn but more useful will save significant time in the long run
money_quotes: Some programmers (although this sort prefers "developer") go so far as to fetishize usability at the expense of utility.
What trouble is it to spend an extra hour learning a somewhat less intuitive code review tool when the programming language whose code you're reviewing required months to learn and years to master?
Instead of focusing on dumbing down our tools, maximizing usability at the expense of utility, we should focus on making powerful tools and fostering a culture of mentorship.
Professional-grade tools are, necessarily, more complex and require skill to use.
We can build better, more powerful, and more productive tools, even if they require a steeper learning curve, so long as we're prepared to teach people how to use them, and they're prepared to learn.
I write tools to maximize the productivity of professional programmers.
key_insight: Programmers should prioritize building maximally useful tools for professionals and invest in mentorship to bridge the learning curve, rather than sacrificing power for approachability.
